competencies.name: Statistical Reasoning
competencies.description: Applies statistical concepts correctly; draws sound inferences from data; understands limitations of methods (bias, variance, confounding); can identify flaws in a study design.
positive_indicators: correctly identifies sources of bias without prompting
explains confidence intervals and what they imply
discusses assumptions behind a model and when they break down
proactively mentions confounders or selection effects
distinguishes correlation from causation unprompted
negative_indicators: conflates correlation with causation
cannot explain p-value interpretation
misses obvious selection bias when prompted
gives shallow or circular explanation after follow-up
over-claims certainty from observational data

competencies.name: Product Judgment
competencies.description: Understands business context and user needs; connects analytical work to product decisions; thinks about impact and tradeoffs; can translate insights into recommendations.
positive_indicators: frames analysis in terms of business outcome
asks clarifying questions about the decision being made
proactively considers downstream impact of a recommendation
translates ambiguous business question into a tractable analysis
negative_indicators: answers stay at the technical level without connecting to business value
cannot articulate who would use the output and why
treats the analysis as the end goal rather than a means to a decision
ignores stakeholder context when asked directly

competencies.name: Coding Ability
competencies.description: Writes correct, readable, efficient code in Python (primary) or SQL; handles edge cases; can reason about complexity; writes testable functions.
positive_indicators: produces working code on first attempt or corrects quickly
handles null values and edge cases proactively
explains complexity tradeoffs
writes modular, readable functions
uses vectorized operations where appropriate
negative_indicators: code fails on basic edge cases
cannot explain time or space complexity
produces O(n²) solution to a problem with a clear O(n) approach
relies heavily on hints to fix bugs

competencies.name: Causal Inference
competencies.description: Understands the difference between observational and experimental data; knows when and how to apply methods such as A/B testing, diff-in-diff, instrumental variables, or regression discontinuity.
positive_indicators: correctly designs an A/B test including power calculation
identifies when observational data cannot support a causal claim
suggests an appropriate quasi-experimental method with justification
discusses threats to validity (SUTVA, exclusion restriction, etc.)
negative_indicators: cannot articulate why randomization matters
does not know what a confounder is
proposes causal conclusions from correlational data without caveats
cannot describe what an instrumental variable does conceptually

competencies.name: Communication
competencies.description: Explains technical concepts clearly to a mixed audience; listens actively; tailors depth to the listener; uses concrete examples; structures a response before speaking.
positive_indicators: leads with the conclusion, then explains
checks whether the explanation landed
uses a concrete analogy to explain an abstract concept
adjusts depth when interviewer signals confusion
negative_indicators: jumps to technical detail before framing the question
provides long answers without a clear structure
does not check whether the explanation was understood
cannot simplify when asked to explain to a non-technical stakeholder

competencies.name: Collaboration
competencies.description: Works constructively with cross-functional partners; receptive to feedback; gives credit; handles disagreement professionally.
positive_indicators: describes a situation where they changed direction based on feedback
gives specific credit to teammates
describes productive disagreement with a colleague
negative_indicators: takes sole credit for team outcomes
dismisses or ignores feedback during the interview exercise
describes conflicts without any self-reflection

competencies.name: Ambiguity Handling
competencies.description: Comfortable operating with incomplete information; makes reasonable assumptions and states them explicitly; asks clarifying questions before diving into details.
positive_indicators: asks 1–2 targeted clarifying questions before attempting a solution
explicitly states assumptions and checks if they are reasonable
makes progress despite missing information
updates approach when new information is introduced
negative_indicators: freezes when the problem is underspecified
makes major assumptions without stating them
asks too many clarifying questions before making any attempt
does not update approach when given corrective feedback
